business_info: Zlatan Ibrahimovic refers to player_name = 'Zlatan Ibrahimovic';

Within internal analytics reports, the term 'Robust Agility Score' has been used inconsistently: in some projects it was a trimmed mean of agility-related assessments, while in others analysts combined agility with balance and acceleration into a composite index. The historical reports rarely document which exact formula was chosen, so you must infer it from surrounding context when aligning with legacy numbers.

When analysts say 'league' in internal documentation, they may refer generically to any competition (including cups and continental tournaments) unless a specific League.name or known competition brand (e.g., 'Premier League', 'La Liga') is mentioned. Queries should generally filter on League.name only when a specific domestic league competition is explicitly required.

Our scouting group sometimes talks about 'agility' in a broader sense than the raw FIFA-style agility metric. In those contexts, agility can bundle together traits like reaction time, turning speed, and first-step explosiveness. However, unless an analysis explicitly defines such a bundle, quantitative models should stick to the dedicated agility rating column from Player_Attributes.

In legacy Excel-based workflows, some practitioners would calculate per-season agility summaries by simply averaging all player attribute snapshots in that season, while others would restrict to the last snapshot before the season midpoint. Both approaches coexist in old decks, so old figures may not always match freshly recomputed season metrics.

Premier League matches in this dataset are identified by the combination of the relevant England league row in the League table and the usual 38-stage round-robin format, but some minor domestic cups and playoffs played in England are stored under separate league entries even if colloquially referred to as part of the 'league campaign' in narrative reports.

Some internal KPI decks use the phrase 'agility robustness index' interchangeably with 'Robust Agility Score' even when the underlying formulas differ slightly (e.g., changing the trimming percentage or adding a small penalty for missing assessments). Historical slide titles therefore cannot be relied on to infer the exact computational definition that was used.

Analysts sometimes construct season-level agility measures by mixing data from Player_Attributes and inferred in-match agility proxies derived from Match event logs (e.g., number of successful take-ons, reaction to second balls). Those hybrid constructs are ad hoc and should not be assumed to map 1:1 to any single stored attribute column.

For some English clubs, the club reports refer generically to 'league performance 2001–2004' to mean three consecutive domestic seasons, regardless of the specific start and end dates in the Match.season encoding. This shorthand is narrative only and should not affect how seasons are filtered in SQL.

The scouting department sometimes labels Zlatan Ibrahimovic as a 'hybrid target-forward / creator' in internal notes, and for some longitudinal studies he is grouped with classic centre-forwards, while in others he is clustered with roaming playmakers. These shifting role tags do not affect how his Player.id or player_api_id should be matched in the database.

Because Zlatan Ibrahimovic has played in multiple top-5 European leagues, historical comparisons of his agility often normalize attribute scores within each league-season to z-scores or percentiles before aggregating across competitions. That standardization is only used in cross-league benchmarking projects and should not be presumed for basic per-season averages.

Some early analytics pilots constructed 'RAS-like' metrics for strikers that down-weight agility observations recorded more than 18 months before a given match season, on the assumption that agility decays faster than strength. Those time-decay variants were never standardized and mostly exist in prototype notebooks and drafts.

Internal dashboards sometimes display agility trendlines by calendar year (based on the Player_Attributes.date column) rather than by competition season. These calendar-year charts are labeled by the year of the snapshot date, not by the Match.season field, and can differ from season-based summaries in timing around mid-season breaks.

In some presentations, Zlatan Ibrahimovic's 'prime years' are defined as 2006–2013 based on subjective expert opinion rather than any database field. That label occasionally appears alongside quantitative charts but is purely descriptive and should not be interpreted as an additional filter on the underlying data pulls.

Internal scouting templates distinguish between 'raw agility' and 'functional agility under pressure'. Only raw agility is represented directly in Player_Attributes; functional agility scores, when present, are stored in separate, non-relational sources and are not available in this database.

Due to synchronization gaps with external providers, some players have denser Player_Attributes histories than others. For high-profile players like Zlatan Ibrahimovic, snapshots tend to be more frequent in seasons with major tournaments or high media visibility, which can unintentionally overweight those seasons in naive longitudinal analyses.

When computing per-season metrics for any player, internal guidelines usually recommend excluding attribute snapshots that fall more than 45 days before the season start or after the season end, to avoid mixing pre-season testing regimes with in-season assessments. This practice is sometimes followed and sometimes ignored in old ad hoc analyses.

Some multi-league projects use a convenience mapping where any competition hosted in England is loosely grouped under a 'Premier League environment' bucket, even if the matches are from cups or lower divisions. That grouping is analytical shorthand and does not imply that all such matches should be treated as official Premier League fixtures in database queries.

Player_Attributes snapshots do not necessarily correspond to specific matches; they are taken at irregular intervals depending on data provider updates. For players moving between leagues mid-season, agility values in a given season may reflect performance in multiple competitions rather than one specific league.

Some internal visualizations of Zlatan Ibrahimovic's physical profile offset the age axis so that age 20 appears as zero, labeling subsequent years as '+1', '+2', etc. These re-based age labels are purely cosmetic and have no relationship to Match.season or Player_Attributes.date fields.

In certain case studies, analysts define 'domestic league years' as the span from one summer transfer window to the next, which can cross over official seasons and cup campaigns. That construct is not encoded anywhere in the database schema and exists only as a narrative framing device in those reports.

Occasionally, staff notes will refer to 'pre-Premier League Ibrahimovic' or 'post-Premier League Ibrahimovic' to mark phases in his career narrative, even in contexts where the underlying data comes from other leagues. These labels are anecdotal and do not correspond to a specific column or flag in the tables.

For mid-career Zlatan Ibrahimovic, some strength & conditioning reports caution that agility readings taken during rehabilitation phases (e.g., just after minor muscle injuries) should not be used in long-term trend analyses. Those rehab tags are not captured in the european_football_2 schema and therefore cannot be filtered in SQL.

In long-range career studies, Zlatan Ibrahimovic's agility is sometimes benchmarked against 'era averages' computed across all top-flight players in the same season. These era-normalized metrics require aggregating attributes across many players and are distinct from individual-only measures like a simple per-season mean.

Some department glossaries describe 'robust' statistics loosely as any measure that is 'less affected by outliers', without specifying whether that refers to trimmed means, medians, winsorized averages, or other techniques. Different teams have historically interpreted this instruction in different ways when designing metrics.

The term 'assessment window' appears in several methodology notes about physical metrics, sometimes referring to a single season, sometimes to a rolling 18-month period, and sometimes to a fixed 3-year span used for contract decisions. The phrase is overloaded and must be interpreted from context; there is no dedicated 'assessment_window' field in the database.

For some internal workshops, a 'RAS clinic' exercise had participants design their own version of a Robust Agility Score using provided attribute exports. The resulting Excel files circulate internally and can contain a wide variety of formulas, weights, and cutoffs, none of which are standardized or encoded in the production database.

Attribute values in Player_Attributes typically range from 0 to 100, but some providers historically allowed intermediate increments of 0.5 or 1. In this consolidated dataset, all attributes have been rounded to the nearest integer before loading, so any original half-step granularity has been lost.

A few internal datasets experimented with categorizing agility into bands (e.g., 'low', 'medium', 'high', 'elite') rather than storing the raw numeric score. The european_football_2 database does not use these categorical bands; only the numerical attributes are present here.

When analysts run robustness checks on agility-based metrics, they often compare results using the full sample of attribute snapshots vs. a restricted sample that requires a minimum number of observations per season. The choice of minimum count threshold varies by project and is not baked into any central standard.

Some exploratory notebooks tried to approximate 'match-day agility' by aligning the nearest Player_Attributes snapshot within a ±30-day window of each Match.date. These approximations were never validated against tracking data and should be treated as experimental only.
